svg:
Session 04: Fundamentals of Cryptography Security Operations Master Class — Cryptography Concepts Overview
Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption
SYMMETRIC (AES) Same key encrypts & decrypts
Plaintext
🔑 Secret Key
AES-256 GCM Mode
5f3a8b2c1d4e9f...
Properties: ✓ Fast (bulk encryption) ✓ AES: 128/192/256-bit keys ⚠ Key distribution problem Modes: CBC, CTR, GCM (best) ✗ ECB mode — never use! DES (broken) → 3DES (deprecated) → AES
ASYMMETRIC (RSA/ECC) Public key encrypts, private decrypts
Plaintext
🔓 Public Key
RSA 2048-bit or ECC
a1b2c3...{encrypted}
🔐 Private Key
Decrypt
✓ Solves key distribution ✓ Enables digital signatures ⚠ Slow — hybrid encryption used RSA: 2048-4096 bit | ECC: 256-521 bit Used in: TLS key exchange, certificates, PGP
TLS 1.3 Handshake Flow
CLIENT Browser/App
SERVER Web Server
ClientHello TLS ver + cipher suites + ECDH key_share
ServerHello Chosen cipher + server ECDH key_share
⚡ Both derive shared secret via ECDHE — all further msgs ENCRYPTED
{Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished} Encrypted · Server proves identity with cert signature
{Client Finished}
[Application Data — AES-256-GCM encrypted]
TLS 1.3 Only: PFS mandatory · 1-RTT · Server cert encrypted · Cipher: TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 Avoid: SSL 2/3, TLS 1.0/1.1 (deprecated) · RC4, DES, NULL, EXPORT cipher suites
Cryptographic Hash Functions
Arbitrary Input "Hello, World!" (13 bytes)
H( ) One-way function Fixed-length Output dffd6021bb2bd5b0... (32 bytes)
Algorithm Output Status Use Case
MD5 128-bit BROKEN (2004) Legacy only — collisions exist
SHA-1 160-bit BROKEN (2017) Deprecated — SHAttered attack
SHA-256 256-bit SECURE ✓ Standard — TLS, code signing, files
SHA-512 512-bit SECURE ✓ High-security applications
SHA-3 224-512b SECURE ✓ Structural diversity from SHA-2
bcrypt/Argon2 variable SECURE ✓ Passwords ONLY — intentionally slow
Properties: One-way · Deterministic · Avalanche effect · Collision resistance · Birthday bound: 2^(n/2)
PKI — Certificate Chain of Trust
Root CA Certificate Self-signed · Built into OS/Browser trust store DigiCert / Let's Encrypt / Comodo / ...
signs
Intermediate CA Certificate Signed by Root CA · Issues end-entity certs Root key stays OFFLINE — Intermediate used operationally
signs
End-Entity Certificate (*.example.com) Subject: CN=*.example.com · Public Key: EC-P256 Signed by Intermediate · Valid: 90 days (Let's Encrypt) to 1 year
Browser Validates: 1. Cert not expired 2. Domain matches SNI <text x="612" y="490" class="small" font-size=9">3. Sig by trusted Intermediate 4. Intermediate chains to Root 5. Root in trust store 6. Not revoked (OCSP/CRL) 7. CT log proof included
DV: Domain only (Let's Encrypt) OV: Organization verified EV: Extended validation (strictest) CT: Cert Transparency log
SOC Analyst Visibility: Encrypted TLS payload is opaque — but SNI, JA3 fingerprints, cert details, DNS queries, flow metadata, and behavioral patterns remain visible for threat detection Watch for: Self-signed certs · TLS 1.0/1.1 · Weak ciphers · Unusual beaconing · Newly-registered domains · Large outbound transfers · JA3 malware matches
Post-Quantum Cryptography — Current Status (2024)
NIST Standards:
FIPS 203: ML-KEM Key Encapsulation (Kyber)
FIPS 204: ML-DSA Digital Signatures (Dilithium)
FIPS 205: SLH-DSA Hash-based Signatures
Shor's Algorithm Threat:
RSA / ECC / DH VULNERABLE to quantum
AES-256 / SHA-256 Quantum-resistant ✓
Harvest now, decrypt later: Record traffic today → decrypt with QC later Migrate to post-quantum algorithms NOW for sensitive data
